key_insight: Natural fractures reshape local stress fields, and the resulting shear-driven stress redistribution determines whether a hydraulic fracture deflects, arrests, or links up with the fracture network.
engineering_relevance: Predicting near-fracture stress evolution can improve hydraulic stimulation, CO2 storage, and subsurface integrity design by guiding fracture trajectory control in naturally fractured rock.
method: Coupled laboratory tests on intact and pre-fractured PMMA specimens under plane-strain loading
Digital image correlation to measure full-field displacement and strain during loading and fluid injection
Poroelastic extended finite element simulations with mixed-mode fracture criteria
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that experiments and simulations reproduced distinct HF trajectories, with intact specimens showing Mode I growth and pre-fractured specimens requiring mixed-mode (Mode I-II) behavior to match observed curvature.
watch_item: Results are based on PMMA analog specimens and controlled boundary conditions, so direct transfer to heterogeneous subsurface rocks will require careful calibration.
paper_title: Local Stress Redistribution Controls Interactions between Hydraulic Fractures and Pre-existing Fractures
